caveats: a: No fetched source measures wage-weighted AI exposure for NAICS 484220.
a: Cargo segments such as bulk liquids, construction materials, refrigerated goods, and heavy equipment have different field and office task mixes.
a: Owner-operators often combine driving and administrative work in one role.
rho: Observed productivity growth does not isolate AI or prove labor substitution.
rho: Implementation can increase service quality or fleet utilization without reducing labor.
rho: Compliance and safety decisions remain accountable to people and the carrier.
e: The frozen firm count is margin-bridged and may include firms outside the intended EBITDA band.
e: FMCSA files cover regulated entities and do not cleanly identify NAICS or transferable enterprise value.
e: Eligibility varies materially by cargo niche and contract structure.
s5: The Census statistic is all-industry and based on 2018 responding owners.
s5: A carrier can sell assets without transferring an operating company.
s5: Small private deals are underreported in commercial transaction databases.
q: No public source directly measures pass-through of AI savings in local specialized trucking.
q: Pricing differs across dedicated routes, project hauling, emergency work, and commodity-linked activity.
q: The estimate excludes demand and implementation effects.
d5: The national freight forecast is a broad multimodal proxy, not an industry revenue forecast.
d5: Tonnage does not equal constant-quality service quantity when haul distance, payload, or specialized handling changes.
d5: Construction and resource-linked segments can diverge sharply from aggregate freight.
o: Automation technology can progress nonlinearly after the older GAO study.
o: The primitive concerns the accountable operating firm, not the number of drivers.
o: Closed-site or fixed-route segments could automate sooner than mixed-road local hauling.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Commercial and industrial shippers buy recurring or repeat local haulage by trip, hour, load, ton, mile, route, or negotiated contract, often with fuel and accessorial charges; the cargo and equipment vary but dispatch, compliance, fleet, and driver workflows are shared.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive private fleets, pure brokers, owner-operators without a transferable operating organization, waste collection, general freight trucking, used-goods movers, and financing or shell entities.
lens.included_activities: Independent local carriers using specialized vehicles or handling processes to transport freight other than used household and office goods, generally within a metropolitan area with same-day return, for external commercial customers.

a.bridge: The ILO task-exposure framework and Anthropic usage evidence are applied to the mixed occupation base of local specialized trucking. Driving, loading, equipment operation, inspection, and maintenance receive low AI substitution exposure; dispatch, routing, quoting, customer communications, compliance documentation, billing, and administrative tasks receive higher exposure. The sources measure broad occupations and Claude use rather than this industry's wage-weighted task opportunity, so error is uncertain and could be upward if digital occupations are overrepresented.
caveats: No fetched source measures wage-weighted AI exposure for NAICS 484220.
Cargo segments such as bulk liquids, construction materials, refrigerated goods, and heavy equipment have different field and office task mixes.
Owner-operators often combine driving and administrative work in one role.
a.change_evidence: A current OEWS wage-weighted staffing table for NAICS 484220 plus carrier time-and-motion data separating driving, loading, dispatch, sales, safety, maintenance administration, and billing would materially revise the range.
a.rationale: Drivers and field operators account for most work and remain physically responsible for specialized equipment and cargo. A smaller but important labor pool performs dispatch, route planning, load matching, pricing, safety paperwork, invoicing, collections, and customer service that AI can partly substitute or scale without proportional hiring.

d5.bridge: BTS/FHWA forecasts total US freight tonnage to rise 50% from 2020 to 2050 and reports trucks carry 65% of freight tonnage. Applying the approximate medium-term trajectory to local specialized trucking gives a modest-growth anchor, then widens for construction, energy, agriculture, industrial mix, and local-haul intensity. Total freight includes other modes, long-distance movements, and general freight, so it may overstate or understate this niche depending on commodity composition.
caveats: The national freight forecast is a broad multimodal proxy, not an industry revenue forecast.
Tonnage does not equal constant-quality service quantity when haul distance, payload, or specialized handling changes.
Construction and resource-linked segments can diverge sharply from aggregate freight.
d5.change_evidence: FAF forecasts filtered to truck commodities and short-distance flows, combined with constant-price shipment or load indexes from a balanced panel of NAICS 484220 carriers, would materially improve the five-year ratio.
d5.rationale: Specialized local hauling is tied to physical production, construction, infrastructure, agriculture, and distribution that are unlikely to disappear, while 2024 specialized-freight output growth was modest. A real demand increase is plausible, but cargo-specific cyclicality warrants a broad range.

caveats: The frozen firm count is margin-bridged and may include firms outside the intended EBITDA band.
FMCSA files cover regulated entities and do not cleanly identify NAICS or transferable enterprise value.
Eligibility varies materially by cargo niche and contract structure.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level audit of a statistically representative sample from the frozen 2,317-firm population using FMCSA census records, vehicle ownership, operating authority, customer mix, and owner-dependence interviews would directly estimate eligibility.
e.rationale: NAICS 484220 is predominantly an outsourced carrier activity, so most firms in the frozen LMM band should satisfy the recurring external-service lens. The eligible share is reduced by captive or misclassified fleets, thin owner-operator aggregations, pure brokers, customer-concentrated operations, and firms without transferable permits, equipment, management, or customer relationships.

l.detail: BLS Industry Productivity (IPS) direct: labor compensation (L02) $18,226.5M / sectoral output (T30) $58,973.8M, year 2022

n.detail: margin 0.1558 (margins.json[484]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Trucking EBITDA/Sales 15.58%); revenue/employee $348,088 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 484220); class <5: 22,514 firms, avg revenue/firm $477,213 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $74,350 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 4,045 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,811,762 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $282,272 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 2,533 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,371,445 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $525,271 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 1,964 firms, avg revenue/firm $8,729,799 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,360,103 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 353 firms, avg revenue/firm $25,347,456 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,949,134 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 174 firms, avg revenue/firm $153,173,322 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $23,864,404 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 2,317

o.bridge: GAO evidence focuses on automated long-haul trucking and finds widespread commercial deployment likely years or decades away, with some scenarios retaining operators. Local specialized freight has shorter, more complex roads, loading sites, equipment interfaces, inspections, and customer exceptions than the long-haul use case, so operator need is inferred to be at least as durable; this likely makes the long-haul proxy conservative for operator retention.
caveats: Automation technology can progress nonlinearly after the older GAO study.
The primitive concerns the accountable operating firm, not the number of drivers.
Closed-site or fixed-route segments could automate sooner than mixed-road local hauling.
o.change_evidence: Commercial deployment data showing driver-out autonomous local specialized loads by cargo type, road environment, loading interface, insurance status, and regulatory jurisdiction would materially change this share.
o.rationale: Even with routing and driver-assistance technology, customers need an accountable carrier to provide vehicles, permits, insurance, cargo handling, site coordination, safety, and exception resolution. Software and autonomy may reduce coordination or driving labor, but are unlikely to eliminate the operating firm for most local specialized loads within five years.

caveats: No public source directly measures pass-through of AI savings in local specialized trucking.
Pricing differs across dedicated routes, project hauling, emergency work, and commodity-linked activity.
The estimate excludes demand and implementation effects.
q.change_evidence: Customer-contract samples and load-level panels linking rate type, rebid cadence, realized revenue, fuel and accessorial treatment, and post-automation cost changes across cargo segments would establish actual retention.
q.rationale: An operator can initially retain savings inside fixed per-load, route, ton, or contract rates, especially when service reliability and specialized equipment matter. Freight buyers frequently rebid, benchmark rates, apply fuel adjustments, or negotiate productivity, while excess carrier capacity intensifies price competition; these mechanisms share a substantial part of gross benefit within five years.

caveats: Observed productivity growth does not isolate AI or prove labor substitution.
Implementation can increase service quality or fleet utilization without reducing labor.
Compliance and safety decisions remain accountable to people and the carrier.
rho.change_evidence: Matched before-and-after operating data from transportation-management vendors and at least thirty LMM local specialized carriers, reporting office labor hours per load and adoption persistence over multiple years, would materially change this estimate.
rho.rationale: AI-assisted dispatch, document extraction, exception alerts, quote preparation, invoicing, collections, safety-file review, and customer messaging can fit existing transportation-management workflows. Fragmented legacy systems, sparse data at smaller carriers, cargo-specific rules, driver adoption, integrations, and the cost of errors limit five-year implementation.

s5.bridge: Census reports that 51% of responding US employer-business owners across industries were age 55 or older. That aging-owner share is converted into a much lower five-year qualifying control-transfer probability after allowing for continued ownership, family succession, closures, and non-transferable owner-operators. The source is not trucking-specific and age overstates transaction likelihood, biasing a direct read upward.
caveats: The Census statistic is all-industry and based on 2018 responding owners.
A carrier can sell assets without transferring an operating company.
Small private deals are underreported in commercial transaction databases.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of Secretary of State control changes, FMCSA authority transfers, equipment-finance payoff events, and private deal records matched to a NAICS 484220 firm roster would materially move this probability.
s5.rationale: A fragmented carrier base and aging business owners create recurring succession candidates, but equipment or authority sales, shutdowns, internal family transfers, and continued owner operation do not all qualify as transferable control events.

principal_driver: A large fragmented operating base performs repeat freight movements with document-heavy dispatch and billing workflows around an enduring physical service.
principal_weakness: Most wages remain tied to drivers and field operations, while pricing competition and cargo-cycle volatility can dissipate or obscure digital-work savings.

run_meta.title: Specialized Freight (except Used Goods) Trucking, Local

sources.supports: Defines local specialized trucking as metropolitan-area service generally returning the same day and reports 32,207 employer establishments for 2023.
sources.title: 484220: Specialized freight (except used goods) trucking, local - Census Bureau Profile

sources.supports: Provides occupational employment and wage structure for the broader specialized-freight industry, used only as an occupation-mix proxy.

sources.supports: Provides task-level GenAI exposure methodology and finds clerical occupations most exposed while job transformation is more likely than complete replacement.
sources.title: Generative AI and Jobs: A Refined Global Index of Occupational Exposure

sources.supports: Reports that transportation and material-moving occupations are underrepresented in observed Claude use and its user survey.

sources.supports: Reports 2024 specialized-freight-trucking hours down 1.9%, output up 1.0%, and employment of 468,100 for the broader industry.

sources.supports: Provides current registration data for motor carriers, brokers, and freight forwarders and distinguishes operating entities from arrangers.

sources.supports: Reports that widespread automated-truck deployment was expected to remain years or decades away and that some workforce scenarios retain operators.
